title: A Dictionary of the English Language

facts.text: The Oxford English Dictionary is published by Oxford University Press, a publishing house of the University of Oxford.
Work on the Oxford English Dictionary began in 1857, but publication did not commence until 1884.
The OED was originally published incrementally in unbound fascicles (instalments) rather than complete volumes.
The original title of the OED was 'A New English Dictionary on Historical Principles; Founded Mainly on the Materials Collected by The Philological Society.'
The title 'The Oxford English Dictionary' was first used unofficially on the covers of the series in 1895.
In 1928, the full OED was republished in 10 bound volumes.
In 1933, the OED was reprinted as 12 volumes with a one-volume supplement.
As of January 2026, the OED contained 520,779 entries and 3,927,862 quotations.
The second edition of the OED (1989) was printed in 20 volumes comprising 21,730 pages.
The longest entry in OED2 was for the verb 'set', requiring 60,000 words to describe approximately 580 senses.
The record for longest OED entry was broken by the verb 'make' in 2000, then 'put' in 2007, then 'run' in 2011 with 645 senses.
The OED is neither the world's largest nor the earliest exhaustive dictionary of a language.
The Grimm brothers' dictionary of the German language, Deutsches Wörterbuch, spans 33 volumes and was begun in 1838 but not completed until 1961.
The first great dictionary devoted to a modern European language was the Vocabolario degli Accademici della Crusca, covering Italian, published in 1612.
The first edition of the Dictionnaire de l'Académie française dates from 1694.
The OED presents word senses in historical order, with the earliest recorded sense listed first.
The Shorter Oxford English Dictionary, started in 1902 and completed in 1933, omits words obsolete before 1700 except those used by Shakespeare, Milton, Spenser, and the King James Bible.
The Concise Oxford Dictionary was edited by Francis George Fowler and Henry Watson Fowler and published in 1911, before the main OED was completed.
OUP policy for the OED prefers the spelling '-ize' over '-ise' for etymological reasons, tracing back to the Greek suffix '-izein'.
Tim Bray, co-creator of XML (Extensible Markup Language), credits the OED as the developing inspiration of that markup language.
British Prime Minister Stanley Baldwin described the OED as a 'national treasure'.
The OED has been criticized since the 1960s for being British-centred.
The OED3 revision began entries in sequence starting from the letter M.
As of January 2026, the OED contained 888,251 meanings and 821,712 thesaurus entries.
The OED's format of using large groups of short quotations from a wide selection of authors influenced later lexicographical works worldwide.
The Pocket Oxford Dictionary was originally conceived by F. G. Fowler and H. W. Fowler to be compressed, compact, and concise.
